source_cards.spark: Biological evolution advances life not by planning the next form but by filtering variation through heritability — and that distinction between variation that sticks and variation that dissolves may be the structural move your system is missing.
source_cards.bridge: Your system's wandering agents produce variation — cross-domain resonances, unexpected structural matches, analogies from far fields. But not every resonance the agent surfaces can actually be inherited by the user's concept: some patterns land, reshape the concept's structure, and carry forward into how the user thinks about their work; others are interesting on contact and then dissolve, leaving no trace. Evolution names this distinction precisely — heritable versus non-heritable variation — and treats heritability as the filter that separates cumulative advancement from noise. Right now your system scores for resonance ('does this match the attractor?') but may not yet have a mechanism for the second question: 'can this pattern be absorbed into the concept's structure, or will it evaporate when the card is closed?' That second filter is what converts a wandering system into a compounding one.
source_cards.source_shape: In Darwinian evolution, random variation is cheap and continuous, but only heritable variation compounds. A mutation that cannot be passed forward leaves no trace in the lineage — it is phenotypic noise, not evolutionary signal. Selection pressure alone does not build cumulative structure; heritability is the second mechanism that converts a one-time match into a lasting change in the organism's architecture.
source_cards.use: Consider designing an explicit 'inheritability signal' into the card or session layer — a lightweight prompt or field that asks the user whether a resonance actually changed something in how they hold their concept, distinct from whether it was interesting. Over time, that signal could teach the system which types of cross-domain bridges produce durable structural shifts versus momentary illumination.
source_cards.limit: The evolutionary analogy breaks at the anchor's most defining feature: evolution has no vision. Natural selection is blind, memoryless, and indifferent to any destination — variation is undirected, and fitness is defined entirely by the present environment. Your system is the structural opposite: the user holds a pre-existing telos, and the wandering is pulled toward it. The agent is not neutral; it is searching inside a field shaped by what the user is already trying to become. The report names this directly — your system is Lamarckian in structure, not Darwinian, because the 'organism' is trying to reach a form the user already imagines. The heritability insight survives this break, but the broader evolutionary frame does not: cumulative structure under blind selection is a categorically different process from vision-anchored incompleteness, and treating them as the same shape would mislead the design.

source_cards.spark: Quantum Darwinism shows that a fragile, half-formed quantum state doesn't become real by resolving itself — it becomes real by leaving redundant imprints across enough independent corners of the environment. A concept might work the same way: robustness through proliferation, not through clarity.
source_cards.bridge: Your system's core problem is what to do with a concept that is too vague to score cleanly but too structurally interesting to discard. Quantum Darwinism names a mechanism for exactly that situation: the concept is the quantum system — indeterminate, not yet resolved. Each domain the wandering agent visits is a region of the environment. When a domain can carry a partial imprint of the concept — when it finds structural resonance even across surface distance — it becomes one witness. The concept does not need to sharpen internally to become usable; it needs enough structurally distinct domains to each hold a fragment of it. Under this framing, the value your system delivers scales not with how well any single agent matches the anchor, but with how many independent domains have each registered a partial, non-redundant imprint — the concept becomes actionable through the count and diversity of witnesses, not through convergence on a best answer.
source_cards.source_shape: A quantum system never collapses into classical reality on its own. Instead, selected states survive by imprinting copies of themselves into many separate, structurally distinct parts of the environment — what Zurek calls redundant records. Observers never touch the quantum system directly; they read one of its environmental copies. Classical robustness is therefore not a property of the original signal becoming sharper — it is a property of how many independent witnesses carry a fragment of it.
source_cards.use: Consider adding a cross-domain redundancy score to your system's output layer — not 'how strong is the best match' but 'how many structurally distinct domains have each independently witnessed this concept.' A concept that registers partial resonance across six unrelated domains is, by this logic, more actionable than one with a single high-confidence match.
source_cards.limit: In Quantum Darwinism, the proliferated imprints are all copies of the same selected pointer state — the environment records one winner, repeatedly, and that repetition is precisely what produces classical determinism. Your system is designed to do the opposite: preserve productive incompleteness by resisting convergence on any single dominant structure. If you apply the QD redundancy mechanism naively, you get the same collapse QD produces — many domains all confirming the same reading of the concept, which would destroy the open-ended generativity your wandering architecture is built to protect. The bridge holds for the insight that robustness can come from proliferation rather than resolution; it breaks the moment proliferation means copies of one selected answer rather than independent partial witnesses each carrying something different.

selection.why_these_cards: Heritability (evolution) and redundant-imprint robustness (Quantum Darwinism) both answer 'what makes a find survive,' but they answer with opposite filters: heritability is a single-line vertical filter (does it carry forward into the next state), redundancy is a horizontal filter (how many independent witnesses hold it). Colliding them forces a question neither asks alone.
selection.spark: Both cards quietly disagree about what 'durable' means — one says durability is being passed forward in a lineage, the other says durability is being copied sideways across witnesses. A concept-advancement system needs both axes, and the gap between them is a measurable signal.
selection.motive: The cushion's hardest unsolved sub-problem is distinguishing a resonance that actually advances the user's concept from one that is merely interesting. This blend builds a two-axis durability test the system can score.
selection.tension: Heritability is vertical and lineage-bound (a trait either propagates to the descendant or vanishes); redundancy is horizontal and witness-bound (a state is real if many independent environments imprint it). They cannot both be the durability criterion — one is sequential-temporal, the other is parallel-spatial.
selection.discovery_path: Card Se2b456 asserts a resonance only counts if it is inherited forward into the concept's structure. Card S57cbdc asserts a vague concept becomes real by being witnessed redundantly across many domains. Both claim to define 'durable,' but one durability is temporal-vertical and the other is spatial-horizontal. They cannot both be the single filter unless something distinguishes a find that scores on one axis but not the other. Pursuing that forced the question: what is a resonance that many domains witness (high redundancy) but that the concept never inherits (zero heritability)? The answer is a real category — the seductive dead-end — and the thesis is that the GAP between the two axes is itself the most actionable signal the system can surface.

blends.thesis: Score every resonance on two orthogonal axes — witness-count (how many distinct domains independently imprint it) and inheritance-take (whether it actually reshaped the concept's structure) — and treat the DIVERGENCE between the axes as the primary diagnostic: high-witness/low-inheritance is a seductive dead-end, low-witness/high-inheritance is a fragile keystone the system must protect from being drowned out.
blends.mechanism: Each surfaced card carries two independent scores. Witness-count comes from how many structurally distinct domains the wandering agents independently landed on the same skeleton. Inheritance-take is a lightweight post-card signal (did the user restate or restructure their concept after contact). The system then plots cards on the witness×inheritance plane and routes by quadrant: high/high = confirmed advance, low/high = fragile keystone (amplify, it is doing real work despite weak external support), high/low = seductive dead-end (flag and dampen), low/low = noise (drop).
blends.emergent_structure: The seductive-dead-end / fragile-keystone quadrant map — the claim that the GAP between horizontal witness-count and vertical inheritance-take is itself the actionable signal. Neither card contains a two-axis plane; each card has exactly one durability axis and treats it as sufficient.
blends.advances_cushion: Directly solves the system's central 'which resonance actually advances the concept' problem by giving it a scoreable diagnostic instead of a single relevance number — and specifically protects the rare cross-domain keystone (the whole value proposition) from being buried under broadly-witnessed but inert matches.

source_cards.spark: Quantum error correction engineers a measurement system that is deliberately blind to the information it protects — it can detect that something went wrong without ever seeing what was right. Your anchor does the same thing: it scores relevance without knowing the answer it's looking for.
source_cards.bridge: Your anchor plays the stabilizer role. It cannot and should not specify the answer the wandering agent is looking for — if it did, the agent would retrieve known things, not discover structural resonances across surface distance. The anchor's vagueness is its stabilizer property: it is sensitive enough to score whether a candidate source is in the right neighborhood, while remaining blind enough to the destination that genuinely surprising paths stay open. Just as syndrome measurement detects deviation from a valid codespace without collapsing the logical qubit, the anchor detects relevance without collapsing the search into the already-known. The load-bearing insight is that both systems require a deliberately engineered blind spot — not as a flaw to eventually correct, but as the precise mechanism that preserves the value of what they protect.
source_cards.source_shape: A quantum computer stores logical information in a fragile superposition that collapses the moment you look at it directly. Quantum error correction solves this by encoding the logical qubit redundantly across many physical qubits, then measuring 'stabilizer operators' — probes that reveal whether an error occurred and what type, while remaining completely blind to the logical content itself. The blindness is not a limitation of the measurement; it is precisely engineered. A stabilizer that could see the logical state would destroy it. The useful information — error type — is extracted from a carefully constructed ignorance about the thing being protected.
source_cards.use: Consider auditing your anchor's scoring criteria the way a QEC designer audits stabilizer operators: ask explicitly whether each scoring dimension could, if answered perfectly, reveal the target concept — and if it could, treat that as a design flaw rather than a strength, because a criterion that sees the answer will pull the agent toward the known.
source_cards.limit: QEC is a correction system built around a known target: errors are deviations from a pre-specified logical state, and the entire architecture exists to pull the system back toward that state. Your system has no target state to restore — productive departure from the anchor is the goal, not a correctable error. QEC's threshold theorem gives a precise, quantifiable criterion for success: correction either outpaces corruption or it doesn't, and you can calculate which. Your system has no analogous threshold; whether a cross-domain resonance is valuable is legible only retrospectively, only to the user, and cannot be formalized. Most critically: QEC's engineered blind spot preserves a logical state that was already known and specified before the measurement was designed. Your anchor's blind spot is meant to allow the logical state — the useful insight — to be discovered for the first time. The shape of the ignorance is similar; the purpose of that ignorance is inverted.

source_cards.spark: Quantum mechanics has spent decades wrestling with whether an identity can survive being transferred into a new substrate — and the answer it keeps circling is: yes, but only if the transfer is also a transformation. Your anchor works the same way: it is not just a target the system aims at, it is the thing most likely to be remade by what the system finds.
source_cards.bridge: Your anchor — the user's stated goal — functions as the sparse attractor the agent wanders inside: stable enough to make the search non-random, loose enough to let the agent reach surprising territory. That is the first point of contact. But the quantum inheritance literature adds a second, sharper point: the attractor itself is not immune to what the search returns. Just as a quantum system's identity is constituted by its relational history rather than a fixed core, your anchor's identity is constituted by the work it has generated — and a sufficiently surprising find can reconstitute the goal, not merely extend it. The third point of contact is retrospective coherence: quantum identity only becomes legible after the trajectory is complete, and the same is true of a concept that has been genuinely advanced — the new version of the anchor makes the wandering look inevitable in hindsight, even though it was not prescribed in advance. The mapping holds because both systems are doing the same thing: preserving enough structure to call it the same entity, while accepting that the structure has changed.
source_cards.source_shape: In quantum state inheritance research, the question is whether a quantum system's identity — its full set of relational properties — can survive being passed into a different physical substrate. The answer is not a simple yes: quantum states cannot be copied (the no-cloning theorem forbids it), so inheritance must be a transfer, not a duplication. What persists through the transfer is not a fixed list of properties but a coherent relational structure — identity as pattern rather than as inventory. The philosophical debate underneath this is whether 'the same quantum system' is even a meaningful category, or whether identity is always retrospectively assigned once a trajectory becomes legible.
source_cards.use: Consider building an explicit 'anchor revision moment' into the system — a point where, after a round of wandering, the user is shown not just what was found but whether the finds suggest the anchor itself should be restated. Framing this as a legitimate output, not a failure of the original brief, would let the system do what the quantum inheritance literature describes: advance the concept by transforming the goal, not just extending it.
source_cards.limit: In quantum mechanics, no-cloning is what makes inheritance metaphysically serious — identity is conserved precisely because duplication is physically impossible, so transfer is always destructive and irreversible. Your system lives in classical information space, where the anchor brief can be copied, forked, and iterated indefinitely without consuming the original. This removes the irreversibility that gives quantum inheritance its sharpness: you can proliferate ten versions of the anchor and reconcile them later, which quantum systems cannot do. The risk this introduces has no analog in the quantum case — version dilution, where the anchor fragments across iterations until no single version retains enough coherence to guide the search. The bridge holds for the identity-through-transformation shape; it breaks entirely at the question of what makes inheritance consequential when copying is free.

selection.why_these_cards: QEC demands an anchor that is deliberately BLIND to its target so it cannot pull the search toward the known. Quantum-inheritance-as-identity demands the anchor itself be REVISABLE by what the search returns. These two requirements strain hard: a blind probe that is also rewritten by its own outputs risks rewriting itself toward the very known-ness it was engineered to avoid.
selection.spark: If the anchor is blind to the answer (QEC) yet gets revised by the finds (P02-005), what governs a revision so the revised anchor doesn't quietly become a specification of the answer — collapsing the blind spot the whole architecture depends on?
selection.motive: The cushion includes the case where 'I have a vague inspiration but don't know how to articulate it' — anchor revision is exactly the articulation moment, but uncontrolled revision destroys the productive blindness. This blend makes anchor revision safe.
selection.tension: QEC's stabilizer is valuable precisely because it can never see the logical content; P02-005's anchor is valuable precisely because it can be reconstituted by what it finds. A thing that both can't-see-its-target and gets-rewritten-by-results is a paradox unless the rewrite is constrained to preserve blindness.
selection.discovery_path: Card Sc29203 asserts the anchor must be a stabilizer — engineered blind to the answer, because any criterion that sees the answer pulls toward the known. Card P02-005 asserts the anchor's identity is constituted by what it generates and should be reconstituted by surprising finds. Put together: revision feeds finds back into the anchor, but finds are partial answers, so revision risks injecting answer-content into the blind probe and destroying its stabilizer property. That cannot both hold unless revision is constrained to update only the QUESTION-shape, never the answer-content. Pursuing what 'updating question-shape but not answer-content' means produced the thesis: a revision operator that imports the structural DELTA between anchor and find as a new constraint dimension, while explicitly refusing to import the find's resolved content.

blends.thesis: Anchor revision must be a stabilizer-preserving operation: when a surprising find triggers a revision, the system updates only the anchor's QUESTION-geometry (what gap it is sensitive to) and is forbidden from absorbing the find's resolved CONTENT — so the revised anchor is sharper about what it is blind to without becoming less blind.
blends.mechanism: On a revision trigger, the system computes the structural delta between the old anchor and the resonant find. It abstracts that delta into a new sensitivity dimension (a new way to detect off-codespace relevance) and rejects any component of the delta that names a destination. The anchor thus gains a new axis of blind sensitivity each revision — accumulating sharper questions, never accumulating answers — which is what lets the user articulate a vague inspiration without the system pre-deciding where it lands.
blends.emergent_structure: The question-content separation in the revision operator — the rule that a revision may import structural sensitivity but is categorically barred from importing resolved content. QEC has no revision (its target is fixed); P02-005 has revision but no firewall against content-leak. The firewall exists in neither card.
blends.advances_cushion: Makes the 'anchor revision moment' — the system's articulation engine for vague inspirations — safe to run repeatedly without the well-known failure of converging on the obvious. It lets the system help the user sharpen what they're reaching for while structurally guaranteeing it never hands them the predictable answer.

source_cards.spark: A network of physics papers predicts which research fields are about to split or merge — not by reading the papers, but by measuring which nodes sit between clusters. Your system's wandering agents may be doing something structurally identical: finding the concepts that bridge half-formed ideas before those ideas have names.
source_cards.bridge: Your user's anchor — an incomplete, half-formed concept — is structurally equivalent to a research field mid-reorganization: the final shape is not yet visible, but the topology of what already exists contains a signal about where it is heading. The 'sparse attractor' in the physics model is betweenness centrality: one metric that sits at the crossing point between what is settled and what is not yet formed. Your wandering agents are doing this same work — they traverse a conceptual space and look for nodes that sit between the user's anchor and something the user has not yet articulated. The agent's resonance score is the betweenness signal: not a conclusion, but a structural hint that a bridge exists here, and that the concept on the other side may be the next cluster to crystallize.
source_cards.source_shape: The paper models scientific progress as a living network: papers are nodes, citations are edges, and research fields are clusters of densely connected nodes. Over time, clusters merge when their bridge papers multiply, and split when internal connections thin and betweenness centrality spikes at a narrow bottleneck. The key insight is that the network's topology announces the coming reorganization before any individual researcher consciously decides to split or merge a field — the structure speaks first.
source_cards.use: Consider scoring each candidate connection your agents surface not just by semantic similarity to the anchor, but by how much it sits between the anchor and multiple other concept clusters — a high-betweenness concept is a candidate attractor, the kind of idea that might pull the user's half-formed work into a new, coherent shape.
source_cards.limit: The physics model is a historian: it requires a fully built citation graph of finished, published papers to compute betweenness, and it trains on settled data to predict future structure. Your system operates on the opposite end — a raw, unarticulated vision with no citation graph, no settled corpus, and no prior topology to measure. The sparse-attractor principle transfers as a design intuition, but the actual computational machinery of the paper does not: you cannot run betweenness centrality on a concept that has not yet generated enough linked artifacts to form a graph.

source_cards.spark: Assembly Theory detects whether a complex object was shaped by selection — not by knowing what the object does, but purely by counting the minimum steps needed to build it. Your system might score conceptual richness the same way: not by understanding a concept's meaning, but by measuring how many primitive moves it takes to construct it.
source_cards.bridge: Your system holds half-formed concepts as structural skeletons — incomplete, not yet resolved, but not random either. Assembly Theory suggests that this 'not random' quality could be made measurable: the minimal number of primitive conceptual operations needed to derive the user's sketch from basic building blocks would function as an assembly index for ideas. A concept that requires many construction steps to reach is, by that measure, already carrying selection history — it has been shaped, even if its shaper cannot yet articulate how. This means your wandering agents could score cross-domain resonance not by matching surface meaning but by comparing construction-path depth: a source that requires a similar number and type of primitive moves to construct as the user's anchor shares structural kinship, regardless of what domain it lives in. The three-point brief then becomes the minimal stored record of that selection history — the compressed encoding that distinguishes the concept from noise.
source_cards.source_shape: Assembly Theory proposes that any complex object carries an 'assembly index' — the shortest sequence of copy-and-join operations required to build it from scratch. Objects that could not arise by chance require many such steps, and that high step-count is itself evidence of selection acting on them over time. The key move is that the theory reads selection's fingerprint from structure alone, without needing to know the object's function, purpose, or history — the construction path is the signal.
source_cards.use: Consider whether the anchor a user submits could be characterized not just as a topic or intent, but as a rough assembly fingerprint — a loose count of the conceptual primitives it already contains — so that wandering agents could weight resonance hits by structural depth rather than keyword proximity.
source_cards.limit: Assembly Theory's formalism works because atomic units and bond-forming steps are objectively defined in chemistry — there is a shared, stable vocabulary of building blocks. Abstract concepts have no such universal primitives: what counts as a 'basic building block' shifts by domain, by user, and by framing, making any assembly index for ideas subjective and unstable as a scoring anchor. There is also a tension with your system's deliberate embrace of productive incompleteness: a deterministic complexity measure could quietly collapse the open-ended wander into a ranking exercise, where agents converge on 'high-index' sources and the generative chaos that surfaces unexpected bridges gets filtered out before it can surprise anyone.

source_cards.spark: Biological inheritance never stores traits anywhere in the genome — a trait only exists when the genetic skeleton is run through an environment. Your system's anchors may work the same way: not compressed insights waiting to be unpacked, but genotypes that only express a phenotype when run through cross-domain traversal.
source_cards.bridge: Your anchor — the user's stated concept or work-in-progress — functions like a genotype: it is a sparse, structured encoding that does not contain the insight the user is looking for. The insight is the phenotype, and it cannot be retrieved from the anchor directly any more than a trait can be retrieved from a gene sequence. The cross-domain wandering your system performs is the environment: it is the context that the anchor must be run through before anything expressive emerges. This means the departure vector — the surprising connection that advances the user's concept — is not hiding inside the anchor waiting to be found. It is produced by the interaction between anchor and domain, and it could not exist without both.
source_cards.source_shape: DNA encodes information in layers — base pairs form genes, genes organize into chromosomes — but none of those layers contain traits. Traits are not stored objects; they are emergent outputs produced by running the genome through a specific environment. The genome is a skeleton that carries no resolution inside itself: the same genotype in a different context produces a different organism, because the phenotype is always a product of the interaction, never of the gene alone.
source_cards.use: Consider framing your system's output to users not as 'what your anchor contains' but as 'what your anchor expressed when run through this domain' — that framing sets the right expectation that the insight is relational and context-dependent, not a retrieval from something already there.
source_cards.limit: Biological inheritance is fundamentally a high-fidelity copying mechanism — its entire evolutionary purpose is to transmit structure across generations with minimal drift, treating variation as noise or mutation to be corrected. Your system inverts this completely: structural drift across domains is the goal, not a defect to manage. DNA's sparse encoding is optimized for stable transmission; your anchor is optimized for unstable, productive traversal away from itself. The inheritance logic cannot model a system where the primary value is generated precisely by departing from the inherited structure rather than preserving it.

selection.why_these_cards: P04-001 says you predict the next concept-cluster by measuring betweenness in a built graph — but the anchor has no graph yet. P06-002 says the system should surface 'departure vectors' — directions the concept couldn't reach alone. Se4c1ba says the insight is a phenotype produced only by running the genotype-anchor through a domain-environment. Three cards, three pieces of one missing mechanism: how to point at a not-yet-existing cluster.
selection.spark: Betweenness needs a graph; the anchor has none. But the genotype/phenotype card says the agents' cross-domain runs ARE the environment that expresses structure — so each wandering pass GENERATES the very graph betweenness needs, and the departure vector is the high-betweenness edge into a phenotype that doesn't exist yet.
selection.motive: Directly addresses 'I have vague inspirations but don't know how to make it live / articulate it' — the blend manufactures the topology that points at the concept's next crystallized form before it has a name.
selection.tension: P04-001's betweenness is a historian's tool requiring a finished citation graph; P06-002's departure vector is prospective and graph-free; Se4c1ba insists nothing is stored, everything is expressed on contact. A betweenness metric (needs prior structure) and a no-storage genotype (refuses prior structure) cannot coexist unless the graph is built live by the act of expression itself.
selection.discovery_path: P04-001 asserts coming concept-reorganizations are visible as betweenness spikes in an existing graph. Se4c1ba asserts the anchor stores nothing and only expresses structure when run through a domain. These conflict: betweenness needs stored topology, the anchor has none. Resolving it: if expression-on-contact GENERATES nodes and edges (each domain-run is a node, each resonance an edge), then the graph is being grown by the wandering itself. Once that graph exists, betweenness becomes computable mid-wander — and the node with rising betweenness between the anchor and several expressed phenotypes is P06-002's departure vector made measurable. The thesis is that the departure vector is the empty high-betweenness slot in a graph the wandering builds in real time.

blends.thesis: The system can name a concept's next form before it exists by growing a live expression-graph during the wander — each domain-run is a node, each cross-domain resonance an edge — and surfacing the EMPTY high-betweenness position (the slot many edges point toward but nothing yet occupies) as the user's departure vector.
blends.mechanism: As agents wander, every domain they express the anchor through becomes a node; every structural resonance between two domains becomes an edge. The system continuously recomputes betweenness on this growing graph. A position with high incoming betweenness but no occupying node is a structural hole — the place the topology is 'pointing at' before any content fills it. That hole is surfaced to the user not as a found connection but as a directed gap: 'your concept is about to crystallize HERE, and these six domains are each leaning toward it.'
blends.emergent_structure: The empty-slot-as-departure-vector — surfacing a high-betweenness POSITION that contains no node yet, as the predicted next form of the concept. P04-001 only computes betweenness on existing occupied nodes; Se4c1ba never builds a graph at all. A measurable structural hole in a self-grown graph is in neither.
blends.advances_cushion: Turns the vaguest cushion case ('I can't articulate it / make it live') into a concrete output: the system points at the unfilled position the user's own cross-domain resonances are converging on, giving them a named target to build toward rather than a pile of interesting cards.

source_cards.spark: The cognitive system humans use to form abstract concepts — not by being taught rules, but by absorbing enough varied examples until deep structure becomes visible — may be the same mechanism your wandering agents are trying to compress into a single pass.
source_cards.bridge: Your anchor — the user's concept held as a sparse, unresolved attractor — plays the role of the abstract concept waiting to be formed. The wandering agents, moving across surface-distant domains, are doing what varied exemplars do in Geary's model: each encounter adds a partial exposure to the anchor's deep structure from a different angle. The 'skeleton without resolution' is not a bug in this reading — it is the necessary starting condition, the same productive incompleteness that lets a learner's concept-formation system stay open to new structural input rather than collapsing too early into a fixed definition. The scoring of structural resonance across surface distance, which is the anchor's hardest unsolved problem, gets a candidate mechanism here: not symbolic lookup, but statistical property extraction — the system recognizes deep structure because enough partial exposures have made the common properties legible.
source_cards.source_shape: Geary's controlled semantic cognition system describes how the mind extracts abstract concepts by accumulating exposure to many instances that share underlying structure despite looking different on the surface. No single example teaches the concept; the system builds it statistically, across varied contexts and modalities, over time. What emerges is not a definition but a felt recognition of deep common properties — the concept as a residue of repeated structural contact.
source_cards.use: Consider whether the system could run agents in iterated waves rather than a single pass — each wave returning partial structural matches that seed the next wave's traversal, building the statistical accumulation Geary identifies as necessary for genuine deep-structure recognition rather than surface coincidence.
source_cards.limit: Geary's mechanism is fundamentally slow and iterative — genuine concept formation requires repeated, varied exposure over time, and the statistical learning cannot be shortcut by a single traversal. Your agents currently operate on one brief pass with no accumulation loop, which is precisely the condition Geary's model predicts would yield shallow surface matching at best, not deep structural inheritance. The bridge holds for the shape of the problem — partial knowledge as operating condition, resonance through structural commonality — but breaks at the question of whether a single-pass system can actually produce what the model requires many passes to generate.

source_cards.spark: A biology curriculum treats students' wrong, half-formed ideas not as mistakes to erase but as the necessary raw material that concept-building works on — and your system does the same thing to the concepts it helps advance.
source_cards.bridge: Your system holds a user's concept in an incomplete state and lets it evolve — that is structurally identical to what NGSS heredity does with student preconceptions. In the curriculum, the 'anchor' is the student's current model, however wrong; in your system, the anchor is the user's current concept, however vague. In both cases, the mechanism of advancement is the same: inherit enough structure from the current state to stay coherent, then introduce a departure vector that moves the concept forward without abandoning what was already built. The NGSS insight that names this clearly is that partial knowledge is not a bug in the process — it is the process. A concept that is already complete has nowhere to go.
source_cards.source_shape: The NGSS heredity framework is built on a counterintuitive premise: students arrive with incomplete, often incorrect mental models of inheritance, and the curriculum does not discard those models — it uses them as scaffolding. Each partial understanding becomes the platform for the next stage, so the concept grows by inheriting structure from the previous incomplete version while departing from it. The progression is explicitly designed around the idea that a half-formed model is not a deficiency to be corrected but an operating condition to be extended.
source_cards.use: Consider making the 'incompleteness' of a user's concept an explicit, visible property in your system — something the system reads and preserves rather than tries to resolve, so that advancement is always framed as extension-from-current-state rather than correction-toward-a-target.
source_cards.limit: The NGSS framework is a designed, deterministic progression: curriculum authors know the canonical endpoint in advance and engineer every stage to move students toward it. Your system's mechanism is the opposite — memoryless, non-deterministic wandering whose path is only legible after the fact, not before. The bridge holds for the insight that partial knowledge is an operating condition rather than a failure state, but it breaks completely at the question of direction: the curriculum always knows where it is going, and your system structurally cannot and should not.

source_cards.spark: The NSF Physics division doesn't do physics — it funds the people who do, by holding a sparse taxonomy of categories that pulls research in without dictating where it lands. Your system and NSF share the same hidden architecture: a structured attractor that enables productive wandering without controlling the path.
source_cards.bridge: Your anchor functions as NSF's funding taxonomy does: a structured constraint that bounds where agents can wander without specifying what they should find. The anchor's resonance scorer is the review panel — the mechanism that judges whether a candidate connection is 'in scope' without requiring the connection to be predictable in advance. The critical insight the agent surfaces is about attractor granularity: if your anchor is too precise, agents converge on obvious matches; too vague, and nothing scores high enough to be useful — the taxonomy's coarseness is a tunable parameter, not a fixed property. The NSF's phase transition from open funding to Frontiers Centers also maps directly: your system may need an analogous shift from broad wandering to deep extension once a strong structural match is found, rather than continuing to wander at the same resolution.
source_cards.source_shape: NSF Physics operates as a coarse institutional scaffold — a handful of funding categories and centers that define a space of legitimate inquiry without specifying what discoveries should happen inside it. Researchers self-organize around these attractors, proposing work that fits the scaffold's shape loosely enough to be surprising. The scaffold's deliberate coarseness is the feature, not a flaw: too fine-grained and it becomes a checklist, too coarse and proposals have no gravitational pull toward coherence. The Physics Frontiers Centers represent a phase shift within this system — moments where broad wandering collapses into deep, concentrated extension on a specific frontier.
source_cards.use: Consider treating anchor granularity as an explicit, adjustable dial — test whether loosening or tightening the anchor's specificity changes the quality of matches surfaced, and build in a mechanism that shifts agents from wide-radius wander to deep-extension mode once a match clears a confidence threshold.
source_cards.limit: NSF is a meta-layer: it funds external human researchers who do the actual wandering, and the taxonomy is an explicit, publicly legible bureaucratic document that humans read and respond to. Your agents are autonomous and your attractor is an implicit algorithmic scorer — the wandering is directed by a mechanism the agents cannot read or negotiate with, which is a fundamentally different relationship between scaffold and wanderer. The NSF analogy holds for the coarseness-granularity shape and the phase-transition idea, but breaks entirely on the question of explicitness and agency: how a human researcher interprets and games a funding category has no analog in how your agents traverse a resonance landscape.

selection.why_these_cards: Geary (S2ac45c-002) says real deep-structure recognition needs many iterated exposures — but the system runs one pass. P03-003 (NGSS heredity) says the partial/wrong current model is itself the platform each next stage extends. P01-003 (NSF granularity) says the attractor's coarseness is a tunable dial and that the system should phase-shift from wide wander to deep extension once a match clears threshold. Together they let a single-pass system fake iteration.
selection.spark: Geary's objection is fatal to a one-pass system — but if each wave's partial matches become the next wave's anchor (NGSS: extend-from-current-state) and the granularity dial tightens as confidence rises (NSF phase-shift), then iteration and the accumulation Geary requires are manufactured inside the session without waiting for many separate user briefs.
selection.motive: Defends the system against the most serious technical objection in the deck (single-pass shallow matching) while serving the cushion's 'take this to the next level' case — staged extension is exactly leveling-up a concept.
selection.tension: Geary insists deep structure cannot be shortcut and needs slow accumulation; NSF's phase-shift wants to COLLAPSE wide wander into deep extension fast once a match clears. Accumulate-slowly versus collapse-fast directly strain — resolved only if accumulation happens in compressed within-session waves, with granularity tightening as the heredity-style platform thickens.
selection.discovery_path: S2ac45c-002 asserts a single pass yields only shallow surface matching; deep structure needs iterated, accumulated exposure. P03-003 asserts each partial model is the platform the next stage inherits and departs from. P01-003 asserts granularity is a dial and the system should phase-shift from wide to deep once a match clears threshold. Chain: Geary's many-exposures requirement can be met within one session IF each wave's surviving partial matches are folded back as the anchor for the next wave (NGSS platform-inheritance) — and to prevent the folded anchor from just retrieving the known, the granularity dial must TIGHTEN with each wave (NSF), narrowing search radius as the platform thickens. The thesis is a within-session iterated-wave loop with a confidence-coupled granularity ramp.

blends.thesis: A single-pass system can satisfy Geary's accumulation requirement by running staged within-session waves where each wave's surviving partial matches become the next wave's anchor-platform (extend-from-current-state), while the anchor's granularity dial automatically tightens wave-over-wave — converting wide shallow exposure into deep structural recognition without waiting for many separate user sessions.
blends.mechanism: Wave 1 runs at coarse granularity / wide radius, returning many partial structural matches. The surviving matches are folded into the anchor as an enriched platform. Wave 2 runs with a tightened granularity dial and narrower radius against this thicker anchor, so it can recognize deeper structure the coarse pass couldn't. The granularity ramp is coupled to rising cross-wave agreement: the more independent waves converge on a skeleton, the tighter the dial goes — automatically executing NSF's wide-to-deep phase shift. The loop stops when a wave returns no new structural delta.
blends.emergent_structure: The confidence-coupled granularity ramp across folded waves — granularity is not a fixed dial but a function of accumulated cross-wave agreement, tightening as the inherited platform thickens. Geary has no granularity control; NGSS has platform-inheritance but no tunable resolution; NSF has a dial but no within-session accumulation loop to drive it. The coupling of the three is new.
blends.advances_cushion: Lets the system genuinely take a concept 'to the next level' through staged deepening rather than one shallow lookup — and answers the deck's strongest internal critique, making the architecture defensible against the charge that one pass can only surface coincidence.

references.title: A Deep Learning Model for Cross-Domain Serendipity Recommendations
How to Evaluate Information Retrieval Systems | by Tarun Jain

novel.resemblance: Standard IR uses multi-dimensional scoring (precision/recall, relevance/novelty); serendipity recommendation work separates surprise from utility; quadrant-based routing appears in service-queue modeling. These share individual ingredients: dual scoring, quadrant classification, damping of low-utility results.
novel.still_new: No hit performs the specific move of scoring on witness-count (cross-domain independent corroboration) versus inheritance-take (post-contact structural restructuring by the user), treating the DIVERGENCE between those two axes as the primary actionable signal, and routing by the resulting quadrant to distinguish seductive dead-ends from fragile keystones. The diagnostic value assigned to the gap between axes — not the axes themselves — is the unprecedented transfer.
novel.reasoning: Every hit either scores on a single relevance dimension, combines popularity with utility in a fixed-weight blend, or routes by queue type. None constructs a witness×inheritance plane where the quadrant gap is itself the signal, nor distinguishes 'high external corroboration / low structural impact' from 'low corroboration / high structural impact' as operationally opposite failure modes requiring opposite system responses.

references.title: A Survey of Techniques for Improving Information Retrieval through Query Expansion

novel.resemblance: Relevance feedback and query expansion (Rocchio, pseudo-relevance feedback, agent-mediated dialogic expansion) all revise a query in response to retrieved content. Sensitivity-aware intent clarification adjusts query scope. These share the ingredient of iterative anchor revision triggered by retrieved material.
novel.still_new: Every hit's revision operator imports content from retrieved documents into the query — terms, vectors, passages, or intent signals. None performs the specific move of a firewall that permits only structural-sensitivity updates (new axes of blindness) while categorically blocking content absorption, so that the revised anchor becomes sharper about what it cannot yet see without becoming less blind. The content-import prohibition as a first-class architectural constraint on the revision operator is absent from all hits.
novel.reasoning: The entire relevance-feedback literature is predicated on the opposite principle: revision improves the query by absorbing relevant content. The blend's move — revision that deliberately refuses content to preserve productive blindness — is a structural inversion of that principle that no hit approaches, let alone performs.

references.title: Social Network Analysis: Structural Holes, Brokerage & Closure
[PDF] Structural Hole Theory in Social Network Analysis: A Review
[2602.21066] The Initial Exploration Problem in Knowledge Graph

novel.resemblance: Structural hole theory identifies gaps between clusters in existing social networks; betweenness centrality measures node importance on occupied graphs; knowledge graph exploration work surfaces structural gaps in existing KGs. These share the ingredients of betweenness computation and structural-hole detection.
novel.still_new: All hits compute betweenness or detect structural holes on graphs whose nodes are already occupied entities. No hit performs the move of building a self-growing expression-graph during a live concept-wander, computing betweenness on that graph in real time, and then surfacing an EMPTY high-betweenness position — a slot with no occupying node — as a forward-directed departure vector telling the user where their concept is about to crystallize. The empty-slot-as-predictive-departure-vector, derived from a graph the system grows itself during the wander, is absent from all evidence.
novel.reasoning: Structural hole theory and betweenness centrality are well-established on static or slowly-evolving graphs of existing entities; the creativity-support application of surfacing a not-yet-existing high-betweenness position as a concept-generation target in a dynamically self-assembled cross-domain graph is a distinct transfer that no hit performs or approximates.

references.title: [2312.06648] Dense X Retrieval: What Retrieval Granularity Should We Use?
[2412.17075] Iterative NLP Query Refinement for Enhancing Domain-Specific Information Retrieval: A Case Study in Career Services
[PDF] Adaptive search query generation and refinement in systematic reviews

novel.resemblance: Dense X Retrieval investigates fixed granularity choices for retrieval chunks; iterative NLP query refinement runs successive refinement passes; adaptive query generation enriches an anchor from prior results. These share ingredients of iterative retrieval, granularity selection, and anchor enrichment.
novel.still_new: No hit couples granularity as a dynamic function of accumulated cross-wave agreement — tightening the resolution dial automatically as independent waves converge on the same skeleton, folding surviving partial matches into an enriched anchor-platform for the next wave, and halting when no new structural delta emerges. The confidence-coupled granularity ramp driven by within-session cross-wave convergence, executing a wide-to-deep phase shift in a single session without user intervention, is absent from all evidence.
novel.reasoning: Existing iterative retrieval work either fixes granularity externally, adjusts it by user feedback, or enriches the query from retrieved content — none ties the granularity dial to a convergence signal computed across folded waves within a single session, making the ramp itself an emergent property of cross-wave agreement rather than a preset or user-controlled parameter.
